Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent this semester. I tried to dedicate about 20 hours a week, but with limited resources at home and my anxiety always creeping up, I found myself studying in scattered bursts rather than structured blocks. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time making notes and rewriting concepts, but fitting that around everything else was challenging. Some weeks I'd be on top of things, other weeks I'd barely manage to open my materials.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines and how I was feeling mentally. When assignments were due, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks my motivation would drop and I'd struggle to maintain consistency. Being 27 and having other responsibilities meant I couldn't always stick to an ideal schedule. The anxiety made it hard to predict when I'd actually be productive versus just staring at my notes feeling overwhelmed.
Q3: Usually it was either an upcoming deadline or a notification about new content that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd missed, which happened more often when my anxiety was high. I'd also go in when I wanted to see discussion posts or check if peers had shared anything useful. It wasn't really a daily habit for me—more reactive than proactive.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more routine, especially when I was actively participating in discussions with my peers. Those 15 posts I made happened mostly during a few weeks when I felt more connected to the course. But that routine never really stuck because my engagement level kept fluctuating with my stress and anxiety levels. The CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns, but building consistent habits remained difficult.
Q5: The discussion forums and reading materials were probably the most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I appreciated being able to read other students' perspectives and contribute my own thoughts—those 29 peer interactions actually helped me understand concepts better. The written resources I could download and annotate were valuable because I could study them offline when I needed to. Videos were helpful too, though I often watched them at odd hours when I couldn't sleep due to anxiety.
Q6: I often delayed watching longer videos or engaging with optional supplementary materials. With limited resources at home, streaming long videos wasn't always practical, so I'd prioritize downloadable content instead. Some resources felt redundant or too basic, so I'd skip them to focus on what seemed more directly relevant to assignments. My low motivation meant I had to be selective about where I put my energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exam preparation. I also valued things that helped reduce my anxiety about the course—like clear explanations or peer discussions that confirmed I was understanding things correctly. If something felt too abstract or disconnected from what I needed to pass, I'd struggle to justify spending time on it given everything else I was juggling.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were the first things I'd cut when I was overwhelmed or pressed for time. Required activities had a deadline attached, which gave me external pressure to complete them even when my motivation was low. Optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, even when I knew it might help my learning. The anxiety made it hard to do anything beyond what was strictly necessary some weeks.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded PDFs and readings early in the semester and studied from those extensively offline. I'd print things out when I could afford to, or work from handwritten notes I'd made from the materials. A lot of my actual learning happened with physical notes spread across my desk, completely invisible to any online tracking. I also used some free external resources I found online to supplement what the course provided.
Q10: There were many times when I was actively studying but the LMS wouldn't capture it at all. I'd spend hours rewriting and reorganizing my notes by hand, which is how I really process information. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with a study partner in person, working through problems together. Those deep study sessions where I was really engaging with the material happened mostly offline, away from any trackable platform.
Q11: Definitely—I'd download lecture slides or readings once, then work with them for days or weeks afterward without logging back in. My internet access is fine, but I preferred having materials saved locally so I could study whenever without worrying about connectivity or distractions. Some PDFs I must have read five or six times, but the system would only show one download. That's just how I learn best with my read-write style.
Q12: Mid-semester was particularly rough when multiple assignments piled up and my anxiety spiked. There were weeks where just opening the LMS felt overwhelming because I knew how much I was behind on. My attendance dropped during those periods—I think it shows in that 54% rate. The CBT sessions helped me develop some coping strategies, but there were still moments when I just couldn't bring myself to engage.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. Fatigue was constant because anxiety often disrupted my sleep, which made focusing during study sessions harder. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those materials rather than tackle them head-on. With limited resources, I sometimes had to choose between course materials and other necessities, which added stress. The competing responsibilities of being 27 with a full life outside university definitely impacted my LMS usage.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd log in and scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things or just too exhausted to process information. I'd watch videos while my thoughts drifted, or read the same paragraph multiple times without it sinking in. Those sessions probably showed up as engagement in the data, but I wasn't really learning during them.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially tasks that felt overwhelming or that I wasn't confident about. My anxiety made me avoid things I thought I might fail at, even though I knew that avoidance made things worse. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but that better day didn't always come. The CBT sessions helped me recognize this pattern as avoidance behavior, but breaking it was still a daily struggle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd have bursts of logging in and completing things right before due dates, which probably made my engagement look sporadic. Without a deadline, my motivation wasn't strong enough to push through the anxiety and actually engage with materials. It's not ideal, but that external pressure was often the only thing that got me moving.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more and reduced my anxiety temporarily. But critical feedback, even when constructive, sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me want to avoid the course altogether. I appreciated knowing where I stood, but I had to be in the right headspace to receive feedback without spiraling. The timing of when I checked feedback mattered a lot for how I responded.
Q18: The course structure influenced my participation quite a bit. When modules were clearly organized with explicit expectations, I found it easier to manage my anxiety and plan my studying. But when things felt ambiguous or when there were too many components to track, I'd get overwhelmed and my participation would drop. Having discussion forums helped because I could engage with peers, which made the course feel less isolating.
Q19: Clear deadlines and well-organized reading materials made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when there was too much content released at once or when instructions weren't explicit. My anxiety thrives on uncertainty, so any ambiguity in the course design became a barrier. Also, having required activities spread throughout the semester rather than clustered would have helped me maintain more consistent engagement.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and checking everything repeatedly without actually completing work—kind of like me on bad days. They might be reading and re-reading, trying to understand but not feeling confident enough to submit anything. Or they could be struggling with the actual assignments while still engaging with the learning materials. High activity doesn't necessarily mean productive activity, especially when anxiety is involved.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show in my LMS activity, but I still managed to pass my exams and assignments. My read-write learning style means I engage deeply with downloaded materials and handwritten notes rather than clicking around online. Someone could absolutely be learning effectively while appearing relatively inactive in the system—the LMS only captures one slice of studying.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, submission times, and which resources were accessed. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the emotional state behind the activity, and whether someone was actually learning or just going through the motions. They can't capture my anxiety-driven late-night sessions or the hours I spent with printed notes. The numbers tell part of the story, but not the whole picture.
Q23: To me, being engaged means genuinely trying to understand the material and caring about my learning, even when it's hard. It's not just about clicking through content or hitting deadlines—it's about mentally wrestling with concepts and making connections. Some of my most engaged moments happened offline, alone with my notes, when something finally clicked. Engagement is internal and emotional as much as it is behavioral.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show my constant battle with anxiety and how much energy it takes just to show up some days. It wouldn't capture the offline studying, the peer conversations, or the times I was deeply engaged but not online. It also wouldn't show how the CBT sessions helped me develop coping strategies that improved my engagement over time. My journey through this course was much more complex than any log could represent.
